column4: One of the oldest and most established open-source physics engines, continuously developed since 2001. Widely used as a physics backend in Gazebo (Classic) and CoppeliaSim. The project uses Bitbucket as its primary repository (not GitHub), using Mercurial historically and now Git.

column4: Installation requires building from source on Linux, which is more involved than pip-based alternatives. The build process follows standard autoconf conventions familiar to Linux developers. Several system-level GL/GLUT dependencies must be installed manually before compilation. Installation completes successfully but requires more steps than modern simulators.

column4: ODE uses a C-style API with return codes rather than exceptions. Error handling is consistent with C library conventions. Invalid inputs generally result in assertion failures or logged error messages.

column4: User manual and API documentation are available but less polished than newer simulators. The mailing list is the primary support channel. Community activity has decreased compared to newer physics engines. Requires C/C++ programming knowledge.

column4: C/C++ library API is stable and well-documented. Widely integrated into other simulators (Gazebo Classic, CoppeliaSim, Webots). Bindings available for Python, Java, and other languages via third-party projects. The stable API has allowed it to remain a standard backend for decades.

column4: Mature, readable C/C++ codebase. Consistent coding style throughout. No formal coding standard document, but style is uniform. Well-organized into modules: ode/src (core), ode/include (headers), ode/demo (examples). Comments are clear and informative.
